rulings.Case Title: RANDALL WHITNEY VS. METROPOLITAN TRANSPORTATION COMMISSION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT METROPOLITAN TRANSPORTATION COMMISSION'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS Defendant Metropolitan Transportation Commission's (MTC) motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted. The California State Legislature imposed the bridge toll increase in the San Francisco Bay Area by passing SB 595, of which the Court takes judicial notice. (RJN, Exh. C; see also Sts. & Hy. Code §§ 30916, 30923.) Under Article XIIIC of the California Constitution, "impose" means "enact" or "establish" and does not include the collection of the alleged "tax." (See Cal. Cannabis Coalition v. City of Upland (2017) 3 Cal.5th 924, 944 n.17 ["'impose' most plausibly means to establish or enact, and article XIII C, section 2 applies only if it is the local government doing so."]; Webb v. City of Riverside (2018) 23 Cal.App.5th 244, 258 ["A tax is imposed when first enacted."].) By statute, MTC's responsibility merely consists of overseeing transportation projects funded by the bridge toll revenues after they are collected and preparing a summary of the expenditure plan under Regional Measure 3. (Sts. & Hy. Code §§ 30914.7, 30923(d).) Because the Legislature and not MTC imposed the tax, Article XIIIC of the California Constitution is inapplicable as the Legislature is not a local government. (See Cal. Const. art. XIIIC, § 1(e) [defining tax as "any levy, charge, or exaction of any kind imposed by a local government"] [emphasis added].) Thus, Petitioner Randall Whitney's first three causes of action-predicated on this article's two-thirds vote requirement-must fail. Petitioner's conflation of MTC with the Bay Area Toll Authority (BATA) does nothing to aid his argument because even if MTC were charged with BATA's duties, BATA's discretion in implementing the toll increase was limited to determining the amount of the increase, deciding when the election would be held, and authoring the ballot question. (Sts. & Hy. Code §§ 30916(c)(1), 30923(c).) Despite these limited areas of discretion, BATA was required to carry out the increase enacted by the Legislature; BATA did not itself impose the increase. (See id. § 30916(c)(1) ["[BATA] shall increase the base toll rate for vehicles crossing the bridges¿"] [emphasis added].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for MTC is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO INDEPENDENT TAXI ASSOCIATION VS. APPLIED UNDERWRITERS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Amend And File Its First Amended Cross-Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANTS APPLIED UNDERWRITERS CAPTIVE RISK, CALIFORNIA INSURANCE COMPANY'S Motion For Leave To Amend And File Its First Amended Cross-Complaint Defendants Applied Underwriters, Inc., Applied Underwriters Captive Risk Assurance Company, Inc. and California Insurance Company's motion for leave to amend and file its first amended cross-complaint is granted. Cross-Defendants may raise any challenge to whether the amended cross-complaint states a cause of action for alter ego liability by way of demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or summary judgment/adjudication. (See Brown v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2018) 28 Cal.App.5th 824, 843 ["The 'better course of action' is to allow a plaintiff to amend the complaint 'and then let the parties test its legal sufficiency in other appropriate proceedings.' [Citation.] Thus, a defendant is not required to make its arguments about the sufficiency of the pleading in an opposition to a motion for leave to amend."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: PHANTHIPA THONGTIA ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Tenative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 6, Defendant City And County Of San Francisco'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Tenative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2) The negligence per se doctrine does not apply because Plaintiffs have not produced any evidence that either the service brake system or the parking brake system was defective, and therefore there is no evidence that the City violated Veh. Code § 26453. (UMF 7, 10.) Further, Plaintiffs cannot proceed on a negligent training theory because they have pled only a single cause of action for motor vehicle negligence. Plaintiffs cannot avoid summary judgment on the basis of legal theories that are outside of the scope of the complaint. (See FPI Development, Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 381 [the pleadings are "the outer measure of materiality in a summary judgment proceeding," not "a ticket to the courtroom which may be discarded after admission. . . . 'The function of the pleadings in a motion for summary judgment is to delimit the scope of the issues . . .'"].) However, there is a triable issue of material fact with regard to operator negligence. That Muni did not find any deficiency in the driver's performance during the incident (Haletky Decl. 8) does not establish that it met the applicable standard of care. Although the Berkstresser declaration is primarily directed to Plaintiff's negligent training theory, Mr. Berkstresser also expresses the opinion that the operator's conduct during the incident was below the standard of care required of a driver of a common carrier operating an ETB trolley coach, which required the driver recognize that the propulsion system of the coach remained engaged despite application of the brakes and to utilize the available controls to disengage or shut off power to the propulsion system in response. (Berkstresser Decl. 19.) That is sufficient to give rise to triable issues of fact as to whether the operator's conduct was below the standard of care and whether operator negligence was a proximate cause of the incident. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: PHANTHIPA THONGTIA ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (part 1 of 2) Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment or, in the alternative, summary adjudication is denied. Res ipsa loquitur applies. The presumption, which affects the burden of producing evidence (Evid. Code § 646(b)), arises when the evidence satisfies three conditions: (1) the accident is of a kind which ordinarily does not occur in absence of negligence; (2) the accident was caused by an agency or instrumentality within the exclusive control of the defendant; and (3) the accident was not due to any voluntary action by the plaintiff. (Brown v. Poway Unified School District (1993) 4 Cal.4th 820, 825-826.) In cases such as this where passengers on a common carrier are injured, the elements of res ipsa are relaxed to account for the heightened standard of care for common carriers. (Irwin v. Pac. Sw. Airlines (1982) 133 Cal.App.3d 709, 715-716 ["The higher standard of care is reflected in a relaxation of the elements of proof for an inference based on res ipsa loquitur against a common carrier. An inference of negligence based on res ipsa loquitur arises in cases where a passenger on a common carrier is injured as the result of the operation of the vehicle."].) The presumption may arise not just from the movement or operation of the vehicle but from "something connected therewith, or in control of the defendant." (Gray v. City & County of San Francisco (1962) 202 Cal.App.2d 319, 326.) Here, it is undisputed that Plaintiffs Phanthipa Thongtia and Somnuk Sangsavang were injured because the propulsion system of the electric trolley bus (ETB) overpowered the brakes such that the ETB could not stop and collided with a parked truck. Because the ETB and its propulsion system are in Defendant's control, application of the res ipsa presumption is appropriate. A common carrier may rebut the presumption of negligence with sufficient evidence to sustain a finding either: (1) the accident resulted from a cause entirely independent of any negligence on the defendant's part; or (2) the defendant exercised due care in all respects wherein it might have been negligent. (Irwin, 133 Cal.App.3d at 716.) Defendant attempts to produce evidence to support a finding that the accident resulted from an unexpected electrical surge. However, the Declaration of George Louie lacks foundation as to the electrical surge and the Declaration of Charles Haletky improperly relies upon hearsay statements about the electrical surge causing the issues with the propulsion system. (Louie Decl. 10; Haletky Decl. 7.) Defendant presents admissible evidence that it exercised due care in certain respects wherein it might have been negligent. Mr. Louie testified to the thorough inspection and maintenance schedule on the subject ETB that yielded no indication that any of the systems or related electrical components were faulty. (Louie Decl. 7, 9.) He also indicated that no such problem had arisen before such that it was not foreseeable. (Id. 11.) Kwan Sing Der, the ETB operator, testified that the boot up of the trolley on the day went smoothly and that no problems arose in the hours leading up to when the brakes became unresponsive. (Kwan Sing Der Decl. 2.) Mr. Haletky testified based on video footage as to the appropriateness of the actions taken by the operator in warning his passengers and surrounding drivers and refusing to move into oncoming traffic to avoid the double-parked truck. (Haletky Decl. 2-8; Kwan Sing Der Decl. 3-5.) As a result, Defendants have established that they acted with due care in the maintenance and operation of the ETB such that the burden to establish negligence shifted to Plaintiffs. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, line 1. PLAINTIFFS WENDY HOUGH, MATTHEW HRUDKA'S Motion To Compel Documents From Airbnb; Request For Sanctions Against Aibnb In The Amount Of $1,717. Pro Tem Judge Jason Yurasek, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The parties shall appear and be prepared to argue the privacy issue raised by Airbnb in response to the subpoena and motion to compel. The Court suggests Airbnb be prepared to submit for review sample records exhibiting the nature of the private information of third parties it seeks to shield from production in this matter. If there is a protective order in place in this case, counsel shall bring a copy for the Court's consideration. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: RUSSELL STANALAND DOING BUSINESS AS STANALAND & VS. THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM , LLP, A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANT THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings The Lamb & Frischer Law Firm, LLP's motion for judgment on the pleadings on the fourth cause of action for fraud and deceit is granted without leave to amend. As the court previously noted, Plaintiff's theory of recovery on the fraud claim is that once Defendant learned that the settlement offers were not memorialized, which was after the parties entered into their agreement, Defendant concealed the fact that Defendant intended to assert that the agreement was invalid when it received the settlement funds. (Complaint, 3:10-5:16; 7:1-24; Order Denying Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication entered 12/19/18.) Plaintiff fails to plead a promissory fraud, deceit, or concealment claim. "'Promissory fraud' is a subspecies of the action for fraud and deceit. A promise to do something necessarily implies the intention to perform; hence, where a promise is made without such intention, there is an implied misrepresentation of fact that may be actionable fraud. [Citations.] An action for promissory fraud may lie where a defendant fraudulently induces the plaintiff to enter into a contract." (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 638.) Here, Plaintiff does not allege that Defendant entered into the fee agreement with no intent to perform. In fact, the allegations of the complaint show that Defendant was performing. Paragraph 7 alleges that the parties entered into the agreement on 3/28/16 and Paragraphs 12 and 27 show that defendant performed the contract and brokered a settlement of the personal injury action. Paragraph 27 specifically alleges that "L&F continued to perform services under the Fee Split Agreement . . . ." Plaintiff does not allege concealment. (See Hoffman v. 162 North Wolfe LLC (2014) 228 Cal.App.4th 1178, 1185-1186 ["As with all fraud claims, the necessary elements of a concealment/suppression claim consist of '(1) misrepresentation (false representation, concealment, or nondisclosure); (2) knowledge of falsity (scienter); (3) intent to defraud (i.e., to induce reliance); (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damage.'"]. There is no concealment where a party honestly enters into a contract, begins to perform, and then along the way determines that it will breach the contract. There is no tort liability for concealing an intention to breach a contract under such circumstances. Applied Equipment is instructive. "But the law generally does not distinguish between good and bad motives for breaching a contract. '[I]n traditional contract law, the motive of the breaching party generally has no bearing on the scope of damages that the injured party may recover for the breach of the implied covenant [of good faith and fair dealing]; the remedies are limited to contract damages.'" (Applied Equip. Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 516.) Since Plaintiff fails to show that there is a reasonable possibility to cure the defective claim (and appears to be relying on an inapplicable promissory fraud theory), Defendant's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: RUSSELL STANALAND DOING BUSINESS AS STANALAND & VS. THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM , LLP, A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Plaintiff Russell Stanaland Dba Stanaland Associates For Judgment On The Pleadings; Request For Judicial Notice
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 12, PLAINTIFF RUSSELL STANALAND'S Motion Of Plaintiff Russell Stanaland Dba Stanaland Associates For Judgment On The Pleadings (part 1 of 2) Plaintiff and Cross-defendant Stanaland's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend as to the fraudulent inducement and negligent misrepresentation causes of action. The litigation privilege bars the claims. (See Civ. Code, § 47(b).) The alleged communications were in connection with identifying competent counsel that could represent the Neumanns in the personal injury action. (Cross-Complaint, 11-16.) In Olsen v. Harbison (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 325, the court held on closely similar facts that statements that "'accompanied the negotiation of terms'" between co-counsel were privileged. (Olsen, 191 Cal.App.4th at 333.) The court explained: As we have repeatedly noted, defendant [co-counsel] made his comments when discussing the possibility of becoming associated in on the Klawitter case. This conversation was part of plaintiff's efforts to bring in experienced counsel to assist on the case, take responsibility for the actual trial, and help Klawitter obtain a verdict or settlement. Had there been no litigation, these comments would never have been made. Remarks made in a setting like this can only be deemed connected to the litigation and subject to the litigation privilege. (Id. at 336.) Hart v. Larson (S.D. Cal. 2017) 232 F.Supp.3d 1128 is distinguishable. Moreover, even if the Court believed that Hart's reasoning is persuasive and could be extended to this case, the Court cannot do so. (See Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior Court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455 ["Under the doctrine of stare decisis, all tribunals exercising inferior jurisdiction are required to follow decisions of courts exercising superior jurisdiction."].) In Hart, the plaintiff attorney sued the defendant attorney for fraud, alleging that the latter made misrepresentations regarding a fee-splitting arrangement that the former entered into. The court distinguished the case from Olsen: "unlike in Olsen, where the statements between the attorneys were for the purposes of 'identifying and securing co-counsel to competently represent' a client, the statements here were delivered for the purposes of negotiating a fee arrangement, presumably after Defendants determined Plaintiff's competence." (Id. at 1140 (quoting Olsen, 191 Cal. App. 4th at 333). The Hart court also noted that "a reasonable inference may be drawn that [the defendant attorney's] purpose in making the alleged misrepresentations was to secure the most advantageous fee splitting arrangement for himself, rather than" to provide the best possible representation to his clients. (Id. at 1140.) It concluded that "the functional context of these communications do not have some connection or logical relation to the action to warrant application of the litigation privilege." (Id. at 1141 (internal quotation marks omitted).) This case is similar to Olsen because the alleged misrepresentations were made in connection with procuring competent counsel to pursue the personal injury action. The misrepresentations were not made after Lamb was already retained. Hart notes this distinction. (Id. at 1140 ["Notably, Plaintiff alleged Larson already had an existing fee agreement with the Storm plaintiffs, and Larson's alleged misrepresentations arose out [of] their discussions on division of the existing fee arrangement."].) (end of part 1) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: RUSSELL STANALAND DOING BUSINESS AS STANALAND & VS. THE LAMB & FRISCHER LAW FIRM , LLP, A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Russell Stanaland'S Motion Of Plaintiff Russell Stanaland Dba Stanaland Associates For Judgment On The Pleadings (Tentative Ruling Purposes Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 12, Plaintiff Russell Stanaland'S Motion Of Plaintiff Russell Stanaland Dba Stanaland Associates For Judgment On The Pleadings (Tentative Ruling Purposes only Part 2 Of 2) The motion for judgment on the pleadings as to the quantum meruit/common count cause of action is denied. In Olsen, the court found that the quantum meruit claim failed where there was an enforceable fee sharing agreement. (191 Cal.App.4th at 330-332.) Here, Lamb alleges that the fee sharing agreement is unenforceable vis-a-vis Stanaland because Lamb was duped into entering into it. In that situation, the case is controlled by the general rule that "attorneys may recover in quantum meruit for the reasonable value of their legal services from their clients when their contractual fee arrangements are found to be invalid or unenforceable." (Huskinson & Brown v. Wolf (2004) 32 Cal.4th 453, 456, 461; see Iverson, Yoakum, Papiano & Hatch v. Berwald (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 990, 996 ["[W]here services have been rendered under a contract which is unenforceable . . . an action generally will lie upon a common count for quantum meruit."]; Munoz v. MacMillan, 195 Cal.App.4th 648, 661 (2011) ["Common law principles of restitution require a party to return a benefit when the retention of such benefit would unjustly enrich the recipient; a typical cause of action involving such remedy is 'quasi-contract.'"].) The motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted without leave to amend as to the declaratory relief cause of action. The claim is not "necessary or proper" within the meaning of Code Civ. Proc. § 1061 because if the fee sharing agreement is unenforceable vis-à-vis Stanaland, Lamb can vindicate its rights under the quantum meruit claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JONATHAN KORZEKWA VS. ENCORE KARAOKE LOUNGE, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Verified Responses To Written Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, June 11, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANTS ENCORE KARAOKE LOUNGE, LLC, MARK ALOIAU, ANTHONY AMBURN'S Motion To Compel Further Verified Responses To Written Discovery The report and recommendation of the judge pro tem is adopted in full. Defendants Encore Karaoke Lounge, LLC, Mark Aloiau, and Anthony Amburn's motion to compel further verified responses to written discovery is denied. (See Britt v. Superior Court (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 849, 862-864; Tylo v. Superior Court (1997) 55 Cal.App.4th 1379, 1387-1388.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
